name: Cobisa, Ajofrin and Burguillos de Toledo - circular route
name_original: Cobisa, Ajofrín y Burguillos de Toledo – ruta circular
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Cobisa traverse the diverse landscapes of the Castilian countryside in the province of Toledo, Spain. The region features a mix of rural roads and trails, offering varied terrain for cyclists. Routes often include gentle elevation changes, passing through plains and areas characterized by vineyards. The proximity to historical sites like Toledo adds a cultural dimension to the cycling experience. Best touring cycling routes around Cobisa The most popular touring cycling route is The Hermitage – Reservoir loop from Argés, a 28.6 miles (46.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 3 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the local countryside and reservoir. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cerro Gordo de la Oliva – Almonacid Castle loop from Burguillos de Toledo, a moderate 27.2 miles (43.8 km) path. This route features varied terrain and passes by historical landmarks. Local touring cyclists also love the Plaza de la Concordia, Cobisa – Cobisa loop from Argés, a 27.1 miles (43.6 km) trail leading through the rural plains, often completed in about 3 hours 37 minutes. Touring cycling around Cobisa is defined by rural roads, open plains, and views of the Castilian countryside. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy to difficult. The routes in Cobisa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 1000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Cobisa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Cobisa?
A: There are over 120 touring cycling routes around Cobisa, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences. The komoot community has highly rated these routes, with an average score of 4.3 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Cobisa?
A: The touring cycling routes around Cobisa traverse the diverse landscapes of the Castilian countryside. You can expect a mix of peaceful rural roads and trails, often featuring gentle elevation changes as they pass through plains and areas characterized by vineyards. While some routes in the broader region can be challenging, the touring routes generally offer a balanced experience.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Cobisa?
A: Yes, Cobisa offers several touring cycling routes suitable for beginners. There are 17 easy routes available, perfect for those looking for a more relaxed ride through the scenic countryside. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Cobisa?
A: The region around Cobisa generally experiences a Mediterranean climate. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are often considered the best times for touring cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and comfortable conditions. Summers can be hot, while winters are generally mild but can be cooler.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Cobisa?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Cobisa are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022588437/cobisa-ajofrin-and-burguillos-de-toledo-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cobisa, Ajofrin and Burguillos de Toledo - circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 26.4 km with moderate difficulty.

Q: Can I find longer touring cycling routes for a full day's adventure?
A: Absolutely. For a longer ride, consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10373736" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Hermitage – Reservoir loop from Argés&lt;/a&gt;, which is 46.1 km long and typically takes over 4 hours to complete. This route offers extensive views of the local countryside and a reservoir, making it ideal for a more extended outing.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while cycling near Cobisa?
A: The region is rich in history. While cycling, you can explore various historical sites. Notable landmarks include several castles such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5453856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Servando Castle&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5417231" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadamur Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The historic city of Toledo, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is also nearby, offering attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1035562" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toledo Cathedral&lt;/a&gt; and the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/648212" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Martín Bridge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any natural features or scenic viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the routes offer glimpses of the serene Castilian countryside, characterized by open plains and vineyards. While cycling, you might encounter scenic spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3370281" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Public Fountain (Parking lots)&lt;/a&gt;, which serves as a viewpoint. The broader Toledo region also features natural reserves like the stunning Ruidera Lakes, though these are further afield.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Cobisa?
A: Yes, among the 121 touring cycling routes, there are 17 easy routes that are generally suitable for families. These routes are typically shorter and have less challenging terrain, providing a pleasant experience for cyclists of all ages. Always check the route details for specific difficulty and elevation to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Cobisa?
A: The touring cycling routes in Cobisa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 50 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the peaceful rural roads, and the opportunity to combine cycling with exploring historical landmarks and the beautiful Castilian countryside.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes that pass by historical castles?
A: Yes, several routes offer views or pass near historical castles. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5304856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Gordo de la Oliva – Almonacid Castle loop from Burguillos de Toledo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that features varied terrain and passes by historical landmarks, including the vicinity of Almonacid Castle. You can also find other castles like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5417231" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadamur Castle&lt;/a&gt; in the area.

Q: What is the typical duration for a moderate touring cycling route in Cobisa?
A: Moderate touring cycling routes in Cobisa vary in length and duration. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31913592" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de la Concordia, Cobisa – Cobisa loop from Argés&lt;/a&gt; is 43.6 km long and typically takes around 3 hours and 37 minutes to complete. Routes like this offer a good balance of distance and challenge for an enjoyable ride.
tips: Ajofrín, located in the province of Toledo, is a charming town with a rich history, cultural heritage, and natural surroundings that make it an ideal destination for a relaxing getaway.

It is said that Burguillos was the winery of Toledo, due to the wonderful vineyards owned by the locals. The small church of Santa María stands out, which was built in 1147, and later reformed in 1633.

The origin of Cobisa dates from the year 1175 when Mozarabic documents appear that attest to the existence of a town near Toledo. Although prior to this documentation, these places were populated by Celtiberian towns that stretched between Madrid and Toledo.

From the ruins of that town, Cobisa was born. Its most outstanding building is the Church of San Felipe and Santiago Apostol, built on an old hermitage from the 12th century.
